title: Pyrus rufopilosa
taxonomy: Pyrus rufopilosa is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rosaceae. This genus, Pyrus, comprises a group of deciduous trees and shrubs commonly known as pears. The species is native to the mountainous regions of central China, where it grows in mixed forests and woodland areas. Pyrus rufopilosa is considered to be a rare and endangered species due to habitat loss and degradation. Its classification at the rank of subspecies or variety is not well established in the botanical literature.

intro: Pyrus rufopilosa, also known as the Chinese pear or red-barked pear, is a species of pear tree native to China, characterized by its distinctive red-barked branches and edible fruit. The fruit of Pyrus rufopilosa is rich in antioxidants and has been traditionally used to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues, fever, and respiratory problems, due to its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Pyrus rufopilosa include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Chinese medicine, Pyrus rufopilosa is often prepared into herbal teas, tinctures, and infusions to treat a range of health conditions, making it a valuable component of traditional Chinese herbalism.
